en.extract:
The great crested flycatcher (Myiarchus crinitus) is a large insect-eating bird of the tyrant flycatcher family. It is the most widespread member of the genus Myiarchus in North America, and is found over most of the eastern and mid-western portions of the continent. It dwells mostly in the treetops and rarely is found on the ground.


Taxonomy
The great crested flycatcher was formally described in 1758 by the Swedish naturalist Carl Linnaeus in the tenth edition of his Systema Naturae. He placed it with the thrushes in the genus Turdus and coined the binomial name Turdus crinitus. The specific epithet crinitus is Latin and means "long-haired" (from crinis meaning "hair") Linnaeus based his account on the "crested fly-catcher" that had been described and illustrated by the English naturalist Mark Catesby in his book The Natural History of Carolina, Florida and the Bahama Islands. Linnaeus specified the type locality as "America" but this was restricted to South Carolina by Outram Bangs in 1898. It is now one of 22 species placed in the genus Myiarchus that was introduced by the German ornithologist Jean Cabanis in 1844. The species is monotypic: no subspecies are recognised.


Description
Adult great crested flycatchers usually measure between 17–21 cm (6.7–8.3 in) in length with a wingspan of around 34 cm (13 in). This bird usually weighs between 27–40 g (0.95–1.41 oz). There is no sexual dimorphism. All adults are brownish on the upperparts with yellow underparts; they have a long rusty brown tail and a bushy crest. Their throat and breast are grey.
Males will sing a three-part song composed of two short whistles: a wheerreep followed by a higher-pitched whee, and a soft low churr. This song is meant to be heard by a mate at short distances, indicating periods of low disturbance and stress typically intensifying just before dawn. It is appropriately named "dawn song" (or twilight song).
In addition to the dawn song, great crested flycatcher also produce various calls, a series of fast ascending huit, huit, huit is given in moments of stress or excitement during interactions of between neighbours. The most characteristic sound is perhaps a single loud whee-eep, called out to communicate between mates or parents and young birds. A faster repetition of this call often signal predators in proximity to nests and young. A rapid succession of harsh-sounds rasps signals alarm or stress often heard during territorial disputes between neighbouring birds


Distribution and habitat
The great crested flycatcher's habitat selection may vary slightly with different populations, but can be most often found breeding in deciduous forests and at edges of clearings and mixed woodlands. They also show a tendency to favour landscapes with open canopy, such as second growth forests or woodlands that have been subjected to selective cutting, and also appears to avoid coniferous dominant habitats such as the Canadian boreal forest.
The summer breeding ground covers all eastern, mid-eastern and parts of central United States, including Northern and Southern parts of Florida, parts of Texas, central Oklahoma, and eastern and central North Dakota. In Canada, it is limited to southern Manitoba, extreme southern portions of the St-Lawrence forest of Ontario, Quebec, northeast Nova Scotia and parts of Prince Edward Island.
Its winter range includes most of southern Mexico and the Yucatán Peninsula, and extends along the coasts of Central America. In southern peninsular Florida, Great Crested flycatcher can be found year-round. They migrate to Mexico and South America, as well as to Florida and the Caribbean.


Behavior


Food and feeding
The great crested flycatcher is primarily an insectivore, with insects and other invertebrates making up for the majority of its diet, but will also consume small portion of small fruits and berries. Despite the "flycatcher" of the bird's name, flies, along with spiders, make up only a small percentage of its diet; it prefers prey such as butterflies, moths, beetles, grasshoppers, crickets, and bees and wasps.
Great crested flycatchers will use a variety of hunting tactics, although the most common method observed is a rather passive sit-and-wait strategy. Perched in high canopies, they search in all direction often accompanied by a characteristic head bobbing. Once they have spotted a potential prey, they swoop down and will pursue if they missed on the first dive. They can also be seen abruptly braking and hovering, picking insects or small fruits off of leaves, trunks or other surfaces, sometimes crashing into the foliage in the process. Fruits and berries, when consumed are swallowed whole and the pits later regurgitated.


Breeding

Nest building begins as early as mid April for populations of the southern distribution, and as late as June for northern populations (i.e. Manitoba).
Great crested flycatchers are socially monogamous with chances of pairs reforming in following years, given that both members of the pair survive the winter.  Pairs have been observed to attempt copulation from the beginning of nest building all the way to the hatching of young, sometimes during the nest building process; this is thought to be a strategy developed by males in order to prevent female infidelity. Mating ritual for the great crested flycatcher is described as males swooping down from a high perch in order to initiate mating with females, sometimes hovering near hideaway if female retreats, before returning to perch and repeating diving routine until mating is successful.
Although both parents will inspect potential sites, building of the nest is done almost entirely by the female, while the male closely guards its mate. Cavities that are large enough in size and opening are the preferred nesting sites, whether naturally occurring or excavated by other species (8) as well as use nesting boxes and other man-made structures.  Most chosen cavities are situated between 2 and 6 m (6 ft 7 in and 19 ft 8 in) off the forest ground (11,15). The nest itself is built within 2 to 4 days and is composed mostly of vegetation and plant fibers, such as grasses, moss, leaves, but also pieces of animal fur and feathers, pieces of shed snakeskin, and artificial materials (ex: strings, tape, cloth, and plastic objects). The inner diameter ranges from 7–9 cm.
Great crested flycatchers lay a single clutch of 4-8 eggs, which are incubated on average for two weeks by the female only. After hatching, nestlings will typically spend another two weeks in the nest before fledging. During this time, nestlings are fed an insect dominated diet by both parents, although females will make more frequent visits.


Gallery


Footnotes

The '''great crested flycatcher''' ('''''Myiarchus crinitus''''') is a large [[Insectivore|insect-eating]] [[bird]] of the [[tyrant flycatcher]] family. It is the most widespread member of the [[genus]] ''Myiarchus'' in [[North America]], and is found over most of the eastern and mid-western portions of the continent.<ref>{{Cite book|editor-last1=Alderfer|editor-first1=Jonathan|year=2008|title=National Geographic Complete Birds of North America|publisher=National Geographic|location=Washington, D.C.|isbn=978-0-7922-4175-1|page=396}}</ref> It dwells mostly in the treetops and rarely is found on the ground.<ref name=Cornell>{{cite web|url=https://www.birds.cornell.edu/AllAboutBirds/BirdGuide/Great_Crested_Flycatcher_dtl.html|publisher=Cornell Laboratory of Ornithology|title=All About Birds: Great Crested Flycatcher|access-date=2008-06-12}}</ref>

==Taxonomy==
The great crested flycatcher was [[Species description|formally described]] in 1758 by the Swedish naturalist [[Carl Linnaeus]] in the [[10th edition of Systema Naturae|tenth edition]] of his ''[[Systema Naturae]]''. He placed it with the thrushes in the [[genus]] ''[[Turdus]]'' and coined the [[binomial name]] ''Turdus crinitus''.<ref>{{cite book | last=Linnaeus | first=Carl | author-link=Carl Linnaeus | year=1758 | title= Systema Naturae per regna tria naturae, secundum classes, ordines, genera, species, cum characteribus, differentiis, synonymis, locis | volume=1 | edition=10th | page=170 | publisher=Laurentii Salvii | location=Holmiae (Stockholm) | language=Latin | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/727077 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor-last=Traylor | editor-first=Melvin A. Jr | author-link=Melvin Alvah Traylor Jr. | year=1979 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=8 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts | pages=202–203 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14500979 }}</ref> The specific epithet ''crinitus'' is [[Latin]] and means "long-haired" (from ''crinis'' meaning "hair")<ref>{{cite book | last=Jobling | first=James A. | year=2010| title=The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | publisher=Christopher Helm | location=London | isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 | page=121 | url=https://archive.org/stream/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling#page/n121/mode/1up }}</ref> Linnaeus based his account on the "crested fly-catcher" that had been described and illustrated by the English naturalist [[Mark Catesby]] in his book ''The Natural History of Carolina, Florida and the Bahama Islands''.<ref>{{ cite book | last=Catesby  | first=Mark | author-link=Mark Catesby | year=1729–1732 | title=The Natural History of Carolina, Florida and the Bahama Islands | volume=1 | location=London | publisher=W. Innys and R. Manby | language=English, French | page=52, Plate 52 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/40753272 }}</ref> Linnaeus specified the [[type locality (biology)|type locality]] as "America" but this was restricted to [[South Carolina]] by [[Outram Bangs]] in 1898.<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Bangs | first=Outram | author-link=Outram Bangs| year=1898 | title=Some new birds from eastern North America | journal=Auk | volume=15 | pages=173–183 [180] | doi=10.2307/4068247 | jstor=4068247 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/27447367 }}</ref> It is now one of 22 species placed in the genus ''[[Myiarchus]]'' that was introduced by the German ornithologist [[Jean Cabanis]] in 1844. The species is [[monotypic]]: no [[subspecies]] are recognised.<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=December 2023 | title=Tyrant flycatchers | work=IOC World Bird List Version 14.1 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/flycatchers/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=7 February 2024 }}</ref>

==Description==
Adult great crested flycatchers usually measure between {{cvt|17|-|21|cm}} in length with a wingspan of around {{cvt|34|cm}}. This bird usually weighs between {{cvt|27|-|40|g}}.<ref name=Cornell/> There is no [[sexual dimorphism]]. All adults are brownish on the upperparts with yellow underparts; they have a long rusty brown tail and a bushy crest. Their throat and breast are grey.

Males will sing a three-part song composed of two short whistles: a ''wheerreep'' followed by a higher-pitched ''whee'', and a soft low ''churr''. This song is meant to be heard by a mate at short distances, indicating periods of low disturbance and stress typically intensifying just before dawn. It is appropriately named "dawn song" (or twilight song).<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Nice|first1=MM|title=Notes on the twilight songs of the Scissor-tailed and Crested Flycatchers|journal=Auk|date=1931|volume=48|issue=1|pages=123–125|url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/auk/v048n01/p0123-p0125.pdf|doi=10.2307/4076978|jstor=4076978}}</ref><ref name="onlinelibrary.wiley.com">{{cite journal|last1=Smith|first1=WJ|last2=Smith|first2=AM|title=Vocal signalling of the Great Crested Flycatcher, ''Myiarchus crinitus'' (Aves, Tyrannidae).|journal=Ethology|date=1996|volume=102|issue=5|pages=705–723|doi=10.1111/j.1439-0310.1996.tb01161.x|bibcode=1996Ethol.102..705S }}</ref>

In addition to the dawn song, great crested flycatcher also produce various calls, a series of fast ascending ''huit, huit, huit'' is given in moments of stress or excitement during interactions of between neighbours.<ref name="onlinelibrary.wiley.com"/> The most characteristic sound is perhaps a single loud ''whee-eep'', called out to communicate between mates or parents and young birds. A faster repetition of this call often signal predators in proximity to nests and young.<ref name="onlinelibrary.wiley.com"/> A rapid succession of harsh-sounds rasps signals alarm or stress often heard during territorial disputes between neighbouring birds<ref name="onlinelibrary.wiley.com"/><ref name = "Taylor"/>

[[File:Great Crested Flycatcher Curry Hammock October 2020.jpg|thumb|right|Great crested flycatcher in Florida Keys]]

==Distribution and habitat==
The great crested flycatcher's habitat selection may vary slightly with different populations, but can be most often found breeding in deciduous forests and at edges of clearings and mixed woodlands. They also show a tendency to favour landscapes with open canopy, such as second growth forests or woodlands that have been subjected to selective cutting, and also appears to avoid coniferous dominant habitats such as the Canadian boreal forest.<ref name="jstor.org">{{cite journal|last1=Fitzpatrick|first1=JWW|title=Foraging behavior of neotropical tyrant flycatchers |journal=Condor |date=January 1980 |volume=82 |issue = 1 |pages=43–57|url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/condor/v082n01/p0043-p0057.pdf |doi=10.2307/1366784|jstor=1366784}}</ref>
 
The summer breeding ground covers all eastern, mid-eastern and parts of central United States, including Northern and Southern parts of Florida, parts of Texas, central Oklahoma, and eastern and central North Dakota.<ref name = "Stevenson"/> In Canada, it is limited to southern Manitoba, extreme southern portions of the St-Lawrence forest of Ontario, Quebec, northeast Nova Scotia and parts of Prince Edward Island.<ref name=BNA>{{cite web|last1=Miller|first1=KE|last2=Wesley|first2=EL|title=Great Crested Flycatcher|url=https://bna.birds.cornell.edu/bna/species/300|website=The Birds of North America Online}}</ref>

Its winter range includes most of southern Mexico and the Yucatán Peninsula, and extends along the coasts of Central America. In southern peninsular Florida, Great Crested flycatcher can be found year-round.<ref name="jstor.org"/> They [[bird migration|migrate]] to [[Mexico]] and [[South America]], as well as to [[Florida]] and the [[Caribbean]].

==Behavior==
===Food and feeding===
The great crested flycatcher is primarily an insectivore, with insects and other invertebrates making up for the majority of its diet, but will also consume small portion of small fruits and berries. Despite the "flycatcher" of the bird's name, flies, along with spiders, make up only a small percentage of its diet; it prefers prey such as butterflies, moths, beetles, grasshoppers, crickets, and bees and wasps.<ref name = "Gabrielson">{{cite journal|last1=Gabrielson|first1=IN|title=The home of the Great Crest|journal=Wilson Bulletin|date=1915|volume=27|issue=4|pages=421–434|url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/wilson/v027n04/p0421-p0434.pdf}}</ref>

Great crested flycatchers will use a variety of hunting tactics, although the most common method observed is a rather passive sit-and-wait strategy. Perched in high canopies, they search in all direction often accompanied by a characteristic head bobbing. Once they have spotted a potential prey, they swoop down and will pursue if they missed on the first dive.<ref name = "Stevenson">{{cite book|last1=Stevenson|first1=HM|last2=Anderson|first2=BH|title=The Birdlife of Florida|date=1994|publisher=University of Florida Press|location=Gainesville|url=http://www.dodpif.org/kiwa/kw-profiles/1994%20Stevenson%20and%20Anderson.%20Kirtland%27s%20Warbler%20%28The%20Birdlife%20of%20FL%29.pdf|access-date=2015-10-15|archive-date=2016-03-05|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160305131141/http://www.dodpif.org/kiwa/kw-profiles/1994%20Stevenson%20and%20Anderson.%20Kirtland%27s%20Warbler%20%28The%20Birdlife%20of%20FL%29.pdf}}</ref> They can also be seen abruptly braking and hovering, picking insects or small fruits off of leaves, trunks or other surfaces, sometimes crashing into the foliage in the process.<ref name = "Stevenson"/><ref name="onlinelibrary.wiley.com"/> Fruits and berries, when consumed are swallowed whole and the pits later regurgitated.<ref name="onlinelibrary.wiley.com"/>

===Breeding===
[[File:RWM 8715.jpg|thumb|Adult in Eastern Maryland with its catch for the nestlings]]

Nest building begins as early as mid April for populations of the southern distribution,<ref name="ReferenceA">{{cite journal|last1=Macdougall-Shackleton|first1=EA|last2=Robertson|first2=RJ|title=Mate guarding tactics used by Great Crested Flycatchers|journal=Wilson Bulletin|date=October 1995 |volume=107|issue=4|pages=757–761|url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/wilson/v107n04/p0757-p0761.pdf}}</ref> and as late as June for northern populations (i.e. Manitoba).<ref name = "Taylor">{{cite journal|last1=Taylor|first1=WK|last2=Kershner|first2=MA|title=Breeding biology of the Great Crested Flycatcher in central Florida|journal=Journal of Field Ornithology|date=1991|volume=62|issue=1|pages=28–39|url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/jfo/v062n01/p0028-p0039.pdf}}</ref>

Great crested flycatchers are socially monogamous<ref name="United States National Museum">{{cite book|last1=Bent|first1=AC|title=Life histories of North American flycatchers, larks, swallows and their allies|date=1942|publisher=United States National Museum|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SgU4G-oWAEUC&q=Bent%2C%20A.%20C.%201942.%20Life%20histories%20of%20North%20American%20flycatchers%2C%20larks%2C%20swallows%20and%20their%20allies.%20U.S.%20Natl.%20Mus.%20Bull.%20179.&pg=PA1|isbn=978-0-486-25831-7}}</ref> with chances of pairs reforming in following years, given that both members of the pair survive the winter.<ref name="BNA"/>  Pairs have been observed to attempt copulation from the beginning of nest building all the way to the hatching of young, sometimes during the nest building process; this is thought to be a strategy developed by males in order to prevent female infidelity.<ref name="ReferenceA"/> Mating ritual for the great crested flycatcher is described as males swooping down from a high perch in order to initiate mating with females, sometimes hovering near hideaway if female retreats, before returning to perch and repeating diving routine until mating is successful.<ref name="BNA"/>

Although both parents will inspect potential sites, building of the nest is done almost entirely by the female, while the male closely guards its mate.<ref name="United States National Museum"/> Cavities that are large enough in size and opening are the preferred nesting sites, whether naturally occurring or excavated by other species (8) as well as use nesting boxes and other man-made structures.<ref name="United States National Museum"/>  Most chosen cavities are situated between {{cvt|2|and|6|m}} off the forest ground (11,15). The nest itself is built within 2 to 4 days and is composed mostly of vegetation and plant fibers, such as grasses, moss, leaves, but also pieces of animal fur and feathers, pieces of shed snakeskin, and artificial materials (ex: strings, tape, cloth, and plastic objects).<ref name="United States National Museum"/> The inner diameter ranges from 7–9 cm.<ref name="United States National Museum"/>

Great crested flycatchers lay a single clutch of 4-8 eggs, which are incubated on average for two weeks by the female only.<ref name = "Taylor"/><ref name="United States National Museum"/><ref name="ReferenceB">{{cite journal|last1=Gillespie|first1=JA|title=Some nestings of the Crested Flycatcher|journal=Auk|date=1924|volume=41|issue=1|pages=41–44|url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/auk/v041n01/p0041-p0044.pdf|doi=10.2307/4074085|jstor=4074085}}</ref> After hatching, nestlings will typically spend another two weeks in the nest before fledging. During this time, nestlings are fed an insect dominated diet by both parents, although females will make more frequent visits.<ref name="United States National Museum"/><ref name="ReferenceB"/>
